_meta.description: Machine-readable version of content/puzzle-bank.md, shaped as PuzzleDefinition records (see plan.md §5.1 / README §17 for the frozen /lib/types.ts contract).

E2.meaning: Easy, one small logical step (off-by-one, one conditional)

M1.meaning: Medium, two chained steps or requires care

_meta.hintDigitRule: hint_digit(puzzle) = ones digit of the puzzle's numeric answer, except SWITCH/STAY -> 1/0 and sf_backspace_compare -> number of Backspace keystrokes used (1). stage_hint = ones digit of the sum of a stage's 3 hint digits. finale_code = stage1_hint + stage2_hint + stage3_hint (concatenated).

puzzles.prompt: Terminal log: [2, 7, 11, 15]. Note: "TARGET SUM = 9. ENTER THE SUM OF THE TWO INDICES." (0 and 1)

hints.level1: Two entries in that array add up to the posted target — look for the pair, not a single value.
hints.level2: Check index 0 and index 1 first; their values already sum close to the target.
hints.level3: Indices 0 and 1 hold the pair that sums to 9.

puzzles.prompt: Board shows [4, 1, 9, 6, 1]. "One packet ID was re-sent. Enter the duplicate."

hints.level1: One ID on that board shows up twice.
hints.level2: Scan left to right and flag the first value you've already seen.
hints.level3: The value 1 appears at two positions.

puzzles.source: NeetCode Best Time to Buy/Sell Stock (Easy)
puzzles.prompt: Ramp ticker: [7, 1, 5, 3, 6]. "Enter the maximum possible gain (buy low, sell later-high)."

hints.level1: Find the lowest price, then the best price that comes after it.
hints.level2: Buy at the lowest point that occurs before a later high.
hints.level3: Buy at 1, sell at 6 — that's the largest gap in that order.

puzzles.prompt: Two file names glow: TRACE and CRATE. "Select the pair if they're anagrams, else select MISMATCH."

hints.level1: Compare the letters in both names, not their order.
hints.level2: Count each letter in both words — same letters, same counts means a match.
hints.level3: TRACE and CRATE use exactly the same five letters.

puzzles.prompt: Sequence board: 0, 1, 2, 4, 5 (range should be 0–5). "Enter the missing node ID."

hints.level1: The board should run in one continuous range — one rung is missing.
hints.level2: List 0 through 5 and see which one isn't on the board.
hints.level3: 0, 1, 2 are there, and 4, 5 are there — one number between them is gone.

puzzles.prompt: Log string: ( ( ) ( ) ). "If balanced enter 1, else enter the index (0-based) of the first bracket that breaks it."

hints.level1: Every opening bracket needs a matching close, in order.
hints.level2: Track open brackets one at a time; if none are open when you hit ')', that's the break — otherwise it closes clean.
hints.level3: Walking the string, every open bracket finds its close before the string ends.

puzzles.prompt: Corrupted buffer arrives on screen as cu#rsor (# = a literal Backspace keystroke). Retype it on the real keyboard, pressing Backspace where # appears, to resolve it to the clean value.
puzzles.hintDigitNote: 1 Backspace keystroke used to resolve the buffer

hints.level1: The # isn't a character — it's a real keystroke waiting to happen.
hints.level2: Type the buffer left to right, and press actual Backspace whenever you hit a #.
hints.level3: c, u, Backspace (removes u), r, s, o, r — resolves to five letters.
hints.level4: The clean value is crsor — and you only needed one Backspace to get there.

puzzles.prompt: Terminal ops: PUSH 5, PUSH 2, PUSH 8, GET_MIN, POP, GET_MIN. "Enter the second GET_MIN result."

hints.level1: GET_MIN reports the smallest value currently on the stack, not the whole history.
hints.level2: Replay the ops in order and track what's on the stack after each POP.
hints.level3: After the POP, only 5 and 2 remain on the stack.

puzzles.prompt: Sorted access list [3, 9, 14, 22, 31, 40]; scanner says higher/lower. "Enter the index (0-based) of value 22."

hints.level1: The list is sorted — you don't need to scan every entry.
hints.level2: Halve the search range each time the scanner says higher or lower.
hints.level3: 22 sits at the fourth position on the list.

puzzles.prompt: Load deltas: [-2, 4, -1, 3, -2, 2]. "Enter the largest possible contiguous sum."

hints.level1: Not every value belongs in the best run — a big enough negative can break the chain.
hints.level2: Track a running sum and reset it to zero whenever it goes negative.
hints.level3: The best run is 4, -1, 3, which sums higher than starting over elsewhere.

puzzles.source: NeetCode Product of Array Except Self (Medium)
puzzles.prompt: Array [2, 3, 4]. "Enter the value that would replace index 1 (product of all OTHER values)."

hints.level1: You need the product of everything except the position you're filling.
hints.level2: Multiply the values on either side of index 1 together.
hints.level3: Index 0 and index 2 hold 2 and 4.

puzzles.source: NeetCode Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters (Medium)
puzzles.prompt: Corrupted key stream: A B C A B C B B. "Enter the length of the longest run with no repeated letter."

hints.level1: Slide a window across the stream and shrink it the moment a letter repeats.
hints.level2: Track the last position you saw each letter; jump the window start past it on a repeat.
hints.level3: The run A, B, C has no repeats before the next A forces a reset.
hints.level4: The longest clean run is 3 letters long.

puzzles.source: Green Book Ch.2, expected flips to first head
puzzles.prompt: Terminal: "A fair relay flips until it hits HEAD. Enter the expected number of flips."

hints.level1: A fair coin doesn't guarantee a head on flip one.
hints.level2: Expected trials for a fair event is one over its probability.
hints.level3: 1 divided by one-half.

puzzles.prompt: Board: two dice, 36 outcomes shown scrolling. "Enter the number of outcomes summing to 7."

hints.level1: List every dice pair, not just the doubles.
hints.level2: For each first-die value, only one second-die value makes the sum 7.
hints.level3: There are six such pairs, one per first-die face.

puzzles.prompt: "A standard 52-card deck. Enter the odds of drawing an Ace, in the form denominator only (1 in ___)."

hints.level1: There are only 4 of that card in the deck.
hints.level2: Odds are total cards divided by favorable cards.
hints.level3: 52 divided by 4.

puzzles.prompt: Bin: 5 blue, 3 red. "Draw two without replacement. Enter the numerator of P(both red) over a denominator of 28."

hints.level1: The second draw depends on what the first draw removed.
hints.level2: Multiply the chance of red first by the chance of red second, given one's already gone.
hints.level3: 3/8 on the first draw, 2/7 on the second.

puzzles.prompt: 3 doors, player picks one, one wrong door opens. "Should you SWITCH or STAY for better odds?"

hints.level1: Opening a wrong door doesn't touch the odds you started with.
hints.level2: Your original pick still only has its original 1-in-3 chance — the unopened other door absorbed the rest.
hints.level3: Switching wins in 2 out of 3 scenarios.

puzzles.prompt: "A vault holds 2 fair coins and 1 two-headed coin. You draw one at random and flip HEADS. Enter the percent chance it's the two-headed coin."

hints.level1: Getting heads is more likely if you happen to be holding the two-headed coin.
hints.level2: Weight each coin's chance of being drawn by its chance of then landing heads.
hints.level3: P(heads) splits evenly between the two-headed coin's guaranteed head and the fair coins' half chance.
hints.level4: The two-headed coin accounts for exactly half of that total — enter 50.

puzzles.source: Green Book Ch.2/Ch.4, weighted expected value
puzzles.prompt: "A trade returns +$12 (25% chance), +$2 (50% chance), or −$4 (25% chance). Enter the expected value."

hints.level1: Not every outcome counts equally here — weight each by its own chance.
hints.level2: Multiply each dollar amount by its probability, then add the three products.
hints.level3: 3 + 1 − 1 = 3 once you total the weighted pieces.

puzzles.prompt: Latency samples: 4, 8, 6, 10, 2. "Enter the mean."

hints.level1: Add them all up, then split evenly across how many there are.
hints.level2: Five samples — total them and divide by 5.
hints.level3: The five samples total 30.

puzzles.prompt: Samples: 2, 4, 4, 4, 6. "Enter the population standard deviation."
puzzles.note: Non-integer answer — round the displayed target to 1 decimal, or swap in an integer-stdev dataset before shipping.

hints.level1: Standard deviation measures how far values sit from the average.
hints.level2: Find the mean, square each distance from it, then average and root that.
hints.level3: The average is 4, and the squared distances average out to about 1.92.
hints.level4: The population standard deviation rounds to 1.4.

puzzles.prompt: Scatter overlay: as trade volume rises, latency rises too. "Select POSITIVE or NEGATIVE correlation."

hints.level1: Watch whether both quantities move the same direction or opposite.
hints.level2: If one rises whenever the other does, that's not a negative relationship.
hints.level3: Both volume and latency climb together on the overlay.

puzzles.source: Green Book Ch.3, symmetric random walk
puzzles.prompt: "A fair random walk takes 10 symmetric steps of ±1. Enter the expected final position."

hints.level1: Every step is equally likely to cancel out a previous one.
hints.level2: A fair walk has no drift — the ups and downs balance in expectation.
hints.level3: Over any number of fair ±1 steps, the expected displacement is unchanged from the start.

puzzles.source: Green Book Ch.3, random walk distribution
puzzles.prompt: "After 4 fair ±1 steps, enter how many of the 16 equally-likely paths end at position +2."

hints.level1: Landing at +2 after 4 steps needs a specific mix of up and down moves.
hints.level2: You need exactly 3 up-steps and 1 down-step, in some order.
hints.level3: There are 4 ways to arrange 3 ups and 1 down across 4 steps.

puzzles.prompt: "Sum every integer from 1 to 50. Enter the total."

hints.level1: Pairing the first and last numbers gives the same total, every time.
hints.level2: Pair 1+50, 2+49, and so on — each pair sums the same.
hints.level3: 25 pairs, each summing to 51.

puzzles.prompt: "In a 12-hour period, how many times do the hour and minute hands overlap?"

hints.level1: The hands don't overlap exactly once per hour.
hints.level2: The minute hand laps the hour hand slightly less than 12 times in 12 hours.
hints.level3: They meet one fewer time than the naive hourly guess.

puzzles.prompt: "20 doors start closed. Pass k toggles every door that's a multiple of k, for k = 1..20. Enter how many doors end open."

hints.level1: A door's final state depends on how many times it gets toggled, not which passes touch it.
hints.level2: A door gets toggled once per divisor of its number — most numbers have an even count of divisors.
hints.level3: Only numbers with an odd number of divisors end open — those are the perfect squares.

puzzles.prompt: "6 traders each shake every other trader's hand exactly once. Enter the total handshakes."

hints.level1: Each pair of traders shakes hands exactly once — don't double count.
hints.level2: Count how many unique pairs you can make from 6 people.
hints.level3: That's 6 choose 2.
